title: American Caesar-Style Salad (Eggless)

subs.name: finely shredded cabbage (patta gobhi) or a mix of spinach and cabbage for crunch
any neutral cooking oil; olive oil can be expensive in India — groundnut oil with a drop of gingelly oil gives a pleasant nuttiness

ingredients.name: tahini (sesame paste)
visual_checks: forms the creamy base of the eggless dressing
ingredients.notes: forms the creamy base of the eggless dressing

subs.name: 2 tbsp thick curd (dahi) plus 1 tsp gingelly oil for a creamier, dairy-based dressing

ingredients.name: parmesan or any hard cheese, finely grated

subs.name: a handful of roasted peanuts (verkadalai) adds a similar salty, crunchy finish if cheese is unavailable

steps.text: Cut the bread into 1.5 cm cubes. Heat 1 tbsp olive oil in a frying pan on medium flame, add the bread cubes and toss to coat. Toast, turning occasionally, for 5–6 minutes until all sides are golden and crisp. Transfer to a plate and season lightly with salt.

While the croutons cool, make the dressing: whisk together the tahini, lemon juice, grated garlic, remaining 2 tbsp olive oil and black pepper. Add water a little at a time until the dressing is pourable — it should coat a spoon thickly.
Taste the dressing and adjust: more lemon for sharpness, more water for lightness, a pinch of salt if needed. The garlic-tahini balance should be forward but not raw-tasting.
Wash and thoroughly dry the spinach leaves — wet leaves dilute the dressing. Place in a large bowl.
Pour two-thirds of the dressing over the spinach and toss gently to coat every leaf without bruising them. Add more dressing to taste.

Pile the dressed leaves onto plates or a large serving platter. Scatter the warm croutons over the top and finish with a generous snowfall of grated cheese and a final grind of black pepper. Serve immediately before the croutons lose their crunch.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: tahini → 2 tbsp thick curd (dahi) plus 1 tsp gingelly oil for a creamier, dairy-based dressing; spinach → finely shredded cabbage (patta gobhi) or a mix of spinach and cabbage for crunch; olive-oil → any neutral cooking oil; olive oil can be expensive in India — groundnut oil with a drop of gingelly oil gives a pleasant nuttiness; cheese → a handful of roasted peanuts (verkadalai) adds a similar salty, crunchy finish if cheese is unavailable
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Tahini (til paste) is available at well-stocked supermarkets or can be blended from roasted white sesame seeds (ellu/til) and a little gingelly oil. Baby spinach (palak) is now widely sold in Indian cities. If unavailable, finely shredded muttaikose (cabbage) with a tighter chop makes a sturdy and equally delicious base. Bread croutons can be replaced with leftover tawa-toasted bread cut into cubes.
